Characterization of human cDNAs which positively regulate the yeast cell cycle

Introduction

A few years ago in Roger Brent's laboratory, Jeno Gyuris initiated a project whose aim was to isolate new human cell cycle regulators by performing a genetic selection in yeast. The selection strategy was based on the ability of a-mating type S.cerevisiae to respond to a-factor, the mating pheromone secreted by the opposite mating type. The binding of a-factor onto its cell surface receptor activates a complex signal transduction pathway and triggers two majors effects : transcription of genes involved in cell fusion (such as fus1 as mentioned below), and cell cycle arrest in G1 phase, just before the Start point. Considering the evolutionary conservation of the mechanisms regulating the cell cycle, it is reasonable to speculate that a human cDNA which would allow the growth of a-type yeast in presence of a-factor would constitute a putative positive regulator of the G1 to S transition.
Upon my arrival in Roger Brent's laboratory, I decided to take over this project with the objective of pursuing the characterization of the cDNAs isolated by Jenš Guyris in order to decide which one(s) deserve(s) an extensive investigation.

Positive regulators of the G1 to S transition

Helen Chertkov built a diploid yeast strain whose two copies of the bar1 gene are mutated (the bar1 gene encodes a protease which degrades a-factor). Therefore, the frequency of occurrence of spontaneous resistance to a-factor in this strain is reduced to about 5x10.
Jeno Gyuris constructed a Hela cell cDNA library in a yeast expression vector which carries a Galactose-inducible promoter. 5x10 transformants were plated onto Galactose plates containing 10M a-factor.
About 450 colonies grew and were unable to grow on Glucose plates containing a-factor. More than 100 plasmids were rescued and restriction pattern analysis showed that 60 different cDNAs were isolated. These cDNAs were optimistically referred to as Prgs (Positive Regulators of the G1 to S transition). Partial sequencing showed that Cyclin C, D, and E were part of the collection. These cDNAs were not studied further.
In order to distinguish between the Prgs which interfere with the yeast cell cycle machinery and the Prgs which affect the a-factor signal transduction pathway, transcription of the chimeric gene fus1-lacZ was assayed in yeast transformed with various Prgs in presence of pheromone. Being interested in proteins that regulate cell cycle, the Prgs which inhibit transcription of the mating genes and thus presumably transduction of the mating signal are of lesser interest and were discarded from further analysis.
Hoping to get some clues on their mechanism of action, a selection of Prgs were expressed in yeast strains where the endogenous G1 cyclins were replaced by human counterparts. The ability of these Prgs to overcome the a-factor-induced growth arrest was then assayed. Some results of this epistasis data are shown in Table I. The results obtained with Prg11 for example suggest that cyclin C and D but not cyclin E are normal targets and/or regulators of Prg11 in human cells.

Table I : Epistasis data for 4 Prgs

Each row depicts the ability of a given Prg to overcome the cell cycle arrest caused by a-factor (+) or the inability to do so (-) in cells with different G1 cyclin genotypes. The first column presents the wild type strain (CLN1+, CLN2+, CLN3+). The 6 next columns present strains that contain either only single yeast G1 cyclin, or lack all 3 G1 cyclins but whose growth defect is complemented by a human G1 cyclin.

CLN+1
CLN+2
CLN+3		 CLN1 CLN2 CLN3 CycC CycD CycE
Prg1 +++ +++ -
Prg11 +-- +++ -
Prg12 +-+ -+- -
Prg56 ++- ++- -


Personal contribution


"Genomics"

The sequence data obtained by Jeno Gyuris being quite fragmentary, I first decided to gather a much larger volume of sequences in order to optimize the outcome of similarity searches. Indeed, new identities and similarities with proteins present in the data base were discovered (Table II). For those sequences which showed no significant similarities, specific sequence motifs such as catalytic domains of protein kinases or phosphatases, consensus phosphorylation sites, or RB-binding domains for example were searched.
In collaboration with the laboratory of Dr. Lap-Chee Tsui in Toronto, a subset of Prgs were mapped on the chromosomes, with the hypothesis that if some of them encode crucial cell cycle regulators, they could be localized at loci which undergo damages in certain human proliferative disorders. So far, no exciting result has been obtained.

Table II : Sequence identities and similarities between Prgs and sequences present in the data bases.

(As found by Blast searches).

IdentitiesSimilarities
Helix-loop-helix phosphoproteinHuman homebox protein Hox-A4
Krupple-like YY1Rate cartilege homeoprotein
HMG 17Mouse KYBP
Hsp90Rate nucleolar phosphoprotein
Glucocorticoid receptorMouse kinesin protein KIF2
Coupling protein G(s) alpha-subunitSplicing factors SC35
ATP synthase A chainHuman FMR-1
Cytochrome c oxidase II subunitBovine alpha-subunit of G(i) protein
Secretogranin IIPig Isocitrate dehydrogenase
8 random cDNA seqeuncesHuman proline-rich protein I
Human desmoplakin I and II
Human PC1/PC3

Comparison with various collections of interactors


In the laboratory of Roger Brent over the past few years, quite a few interactor hunts were performed against different baits such as LexA-cyclin C, -cyclin D, -cyclin E (work of Andy Mendelsohn), or LexA-p53 (work of Jennifer Pietenpol and Jeno Gyuris). With the hypothesis that some Prgs could be present in these collections, I dot blotted hundreds of interactors and probed them with Prgs. No positive result was obtained.

Swi4/Swi6 complementation experiments

As showed in Table II, a lot of Prgs encode transcription factors. The yeast Swi4/Swi6 transcription factor plays a central role in the regulation of Start by transcribing the G1 cyclin genes cln1 and cln2 . Obviously, a human cDNA encoding a protein able to boost the Swi4/Swi6 activity is likely to behave as a Prg by elevating the level of Cln1 and Cln2 in the cell.
Among the genes transcribed by Swi4/Swi6 is the HO gene encoding the endonuclease responsible for mating type switching. I obtained from Linda Breeden yeast strains mutated on swi4 or swi6 and harboring a ho-lacZ reporter gene, allowing to assay the Swi4/Swi6 activity by measuring the b-galactosidase activity in the cells. The subset of Prgs encoding transcription factors were transformed into the swi4 and the wild type parental strain and the b-galactosidase activity measured in cells grown in glucose- and galactose-containing medium (Table III). Prg10, encoding a truncated version of the transcription factor YY1, induces a 10 fold-increase of the Swi4/Swi6 activity in the wild type strain but shows no effect in the swi4 strain. This intriguing result suggests that (a truncated version of) YY1 boosts the Swi4/Swi6 activity by acting on Swi4.

Table III : Activity of Swi4/Swi6 in a wild type and a swi4-strain upon expression of certain Prgs.

 The relative values reported are the measure of the beta-galactosidase activity.
0 means that the empty Prg vector was introduced. In this experiment, the Swi4 cDNA is not under the control of a Galactose-inducible promoter. All the values are normalized to the negative control.

Wild type strain:
0Swi4Prg10Prg12 Prg 467
Glucose11.50.90.9 1
Galactose11.69.610.9

swi4-strain:
0Swi 4Prg 10Prg 12 Prg 467
Glucose115001.80.5 0.6
Galactose11001.31.5 1.1

Interaction Mating Assays

The version of the two hybrid system developed in the laboratory of Roger Brent (Gyuris et al. 1993) has been sent to more than 1000 scientists throughout the world, who were kindly asked to send us their LexA-bait construct. Thanks to an extension of the two-hybrid technology, the interaction mating assay (Finley and Brent 1994), we are now in the position of determining easily whether a given protein interacts with a panel of more than 500 different baits. As this bait collection comprises most of the known cell cycle regulators, I decided to perform mating assays with some Prgs, with the idea that every positive result would bring a direct evidence supporting the involvement of a Prg in cell cycle regulation.
I subcloned a first set of 13 Prgs into our prey vector in which the cDNAs are fused to the B42 transcription activation domain. Interactions between 7 Prgs and all the baits of our collection were examined. Table IV summarizes some of the results obtained.

Table IV : Interaction mating assay results obtained with 3 Prgs and 75 LexA-cell cycle regulators.

The strength of interaction is indicated by plus signs, as judged by the intensity of the blue color phenotype on the appropriate indicator plates (a medium sensitivity LexAop-lacZ reporter gene was used). These results were obtained in at least two independent experiments.
Prg 1Prg 11Prg 38
Hs Cyclin C (+++)Hs p16ink4 (+)Hs p16ink4 (+)
Hs cyclin D (+)Hs Cdk4 (+)
Hs p21 (+++)Hs Cdk6 (+)
Dm Rux (++)
Dm Cdi11 (+)

And Now...

I am currently trying to establish the biological relevance of these mating assay results. In the first place, I confirmed some of these interactions by performing in vitro binding assays where Prgs and/or their interactors were expressed in bacteria as Gst-fusion proteins and the interacting partners were translated in vitro . The interaction between Prg11 and at least 2 members of the Cdk family suggests that this Prg might constitute a novel substrate for these kinases. Barak Cohen in our lab prepared bacculovirus extracts containing human Cdk2-Cyclin A, Cdk2-Cyclin E and Cdk4-Cyclin D kinases. I recently showed that a bacterially expressed Gst-Prg11 fusion protein was heavily phosphorylated in vitro by Cdk2-Cyclin E and Cdk2-Cyclin A extracts, and to a lesser extend by Cdk4-Cyclin D extracts. Interestingly, the sequence of Prg11 exhibits multiple putative Cdk phosphorylation sites. I will devote most of my upcoming efforts trying to study the biological relevance of this phosphorylation. Until I extend further these intriguing observations, please forgive me for hiding the identity of Prg11...

References

Finley,R.L. and Brent,R. 1994. "Interaction mating reveals binary and ternary connections between Drosophila cell cycle regulators". Proc.Natl.Acad.Sci.USA 91, 12980-12984.

Gyuris,J., Golemis, E., Chertkov,H. and Brent,R. 1993. "Cdi1, a human G1 and S phase protein phosphatase that associates with Cdk2". Cell 75, 791-803.
